title: The Docket - Week 3
subject: A judge just banned AI from her courtroom. Her reasoning is worth reading.
preheader: Plus: the argument gap analyzer that caught what two associates missed
bodyHtml:
The Docket

LegalTech AI Hub · Week of April 15, 2026

A Federal judge in the Northern District of Texas issued a standing order that might be the most thoughtful thing anyone in the judiciary has written about AI. Plus three more stories.

👩‍⚖️ Judge Martinez’s AI Standing Order Sets a New Standard

Unlike the blunt “disclose or else” orders we’ve seen, Judge Martinez’s 12-page standing order differentiates between AI used for research, AI used for drafting, and AI used for analysis. Each category has different disclosure requirements. The order also explicitly permits AI for “ministerial tasks” like formatting and citation checking without disclosure. It’s nuanced, practical, and should be the template. Read our analysis.

📉 Harvey Lays Off 15% of Staff Despite $100M Raise

The legal AI darling cut staff just six months after a massive fundraise. The official line is “strategic realignment.” The read-between-the-lines version: enterprise sales cycles in legal are longer and harder than the pitch deck suggested. This isn’t a Harvey-specific problem — it’s the legal AI sales problem. Firms are interested; procurement is glacial.

🔍 Google Adds Legal Research Features to Gemini

Google quietly rolled out legal-specific research capabilities in Gemini Advanced, including case law citation and statutory analysis. It’s not Westlaw yet, but it’s free. The question isn’t whether it’s good enough — it’s whether “good enough and free” disrupts the $10B legal research market from the bottom up. See our tool comparison.

🎓 Law Schools Adding AI to Required Curriculum

Stanford, Georgetown, and Michigan Law have all announced required AI literacy courses starting Fall 2026. The courses won’t teach students to code — they’ll teach them to evaluate, prompt, and supervise AI tools. This is the right approach. The lawyers of 2030 don’t need to build AI; they need to know when it’s wrong.

Tool Spotlight

Argument Gap Analyzer

Upload a brief or motion and our tool identifies logical gaps, unsupported assertions, and counterarguments you haven’t addressed. A litigation partner at a 200-attorney firm told us it caught an issue in an appellate brief that two associates had reviewed and missed. That’s the kind of second opinion that changes outcomes.

Analyze Your Arguments →

Until next week. Keep your clauses tight and your disclosures tighter.
